title: Avoid layout shift with will-change
desc: Apply will-change:transform only while an element is actively animating, then remove it after. Keeping it permanently can bloat GPU memory.
descAr: طبّق will-change:transform فقط أثناء الحركة الفعلية للعنصر، ثم أزله بعدها. تركه دائماً يستهلك ذاكرة GPU بلا فائدة.
summary: will-change on every card sounds like a performance win, but it actually allocates GPU layers for each element permanently, ballooning memory and causing jank. Apply it only during active animation.
problem: Overusing will-change on dozens of elements simultaneously forces the browser to allocate GPU layers for each one, consuming memory and potentially causing repaints on every frame even when nothing is animating. Layout shifts happen when composite layers change dimensions. A common anti-pattern is adding will-change: transform in CSS on every card "just in case" — but this tells the browser to reserve GPU memory for elements that may never animate.
solution: Apply will-change: transform via JavaScript just before the animation starts (e.g. pointerenter or rAF loop start) and remove it in the onfinish callback. For scroll-based animations, use IntersectionObserver to toggle will-change only for visible elements. This keeps GPU layer count proportional to active animations, not to total DOM elements.
solutionTitle: Toggle will-change only during active animation

callout.text: Key idea: will-change is a hint to the browser about upcoming changes. Once the change is done, remove the hint so the browser can reclaim resources.

before.desc: GPU memory ~120MB extra. Scroll jank on every frame.
before.code: /* CSS — bad: permanent on all cards */ .card { will-change: transform, opacity; }

after.desc: GPU memory at baseline. Smooth 60fps scroll.
after.code: // JS — good: toggle on hover el.addEventListener('mouseenter', () => { el.style.willChange = 'transform'; }); el.addEventListener('mouseleave', () => { el.style.willChange = 'auto'; });
